title: Model Compression & Pruning
summary: Three approaches: regularization-driven sparsity during training, post-hoc pruning loop, knowledge distillation. Path from research prototype to deployable product.
key_insights: L1 regularization pushes weights toward zero — prune near-zero after training
Structured sparsity: zero out entire heads or FFN neurons — hardware-friendly
Post-hoc: measure contribution, remove, fine-tune, iterate
Quantization (separate technique): float32 → int8 for faster inference
These tools bridge research → deployable product at acceptable cost

mathematical_core.l1_regularization: L_total = L_task + λ Σ|θ_i| — L1 penalty drives weights to exact zero
mathematical_core.structured_pruning: Remove entire rows/columns of W matrices (heads, neurons) based on importance score
mathematical_core.importance_scores: By magnitude: ||w||, by gradient: ||∂L/∂w||, by Fisher: E[(∂L/∂w)²], by removal impact: ΔL when removed

derivations.title: L1 vs L2 regularization — why L1 creates exact sparsity

steps.name: L2 gradient
steps.math: ∂/∂θ (λθ²) = 2λθ. Force is proportional to θ — shrinks large weights more, but NEVER pushes to exact zero. All weights remain non-zero.

steps.name: L1 gradient
steps.math: ∂/∂θ (λ|θ|) = λ·sign(θ). Constant force regardless of magnitude. When weight is near zero, the constant force can push it TO zero. Creates exact sparsity.

steps.name: Geometric interpretation
steps.math: L1 constraint region is a diamond (corners at axes). L2 is a circle. The loss function's level curves are more likely to touch the diamond at a corner (axis = zero coordinate) than the circle at an axis.

derivations.consequence: L1 naturally produces sparse solutions. L2 produces small but non-zero weights. For pruning, L1 is preferred because it identifies which weights can be removed.

derivations.title: Quantization — precision reduction math

steps.name: Float32 → Int8 mapping
steps.math: q = round(w/s + z), where s = scale = (w_max - w_min) / 255, z = zero_point. Dequantize: w ≈ s · (q - z).

steps.name: Error bound
steps.math: Maximum quantization error per weight: ε = s/2 = (w_max - w_min) / 510. For typical weight range [-1, 1]: ε ≈ 0.004 per weight.

steps.name: Speedup
steps.math: Int8 matmul is 2-4× faster than float32 on modern hardware (GPU tensor cores, CPU VNNI). Memory: 4× reduction (4 bytes → 1 byte per weight).

derivations.consequence: 8-bit quantization gives ~4× memory reduction and 2-4× speed with typically <1% accuracy loss. Post-training quantization (no retraining needed) works for most models.

application_relevance: Directly relevant to sellable systems: customers need models within latency/cost/hardware constraints. Compression bridges 'works in lab' → 'deployable product'.
notes: Diagnostic monitors from transformer calculations doc (sparsity ratios, attention entropy, head contribution) are directly usable as pruning signals
